source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. (unspecified), p. 64, Box 2: Front-End Treasury Richness
raw_chunk_text:
Box 2. Front-End Treasury Richness
Average Sharpe ratios are especially high at short maturities, and the reward–risk
curve is very steep at the front end (with longer maturities providing higher retu
rule_text: Extend duration from short-maturity Treasury bills into longer maturities when the reward-risk curve is steep and natural holders are under-optimizing for return, creating a persistent richness at the front end.
entry_conditions: 1-month Treasury Sharpe ratio > 90th percentile vs. 10-year Treasury Sharpe ratio
Reward-risk curve slope (10Y return - 1M return) / (10Y duration - 1M duration) > +0.15
No acute liquidity stress or flight-to-quality event in past 3 months
exit_conditions: 1-month Sharpe ratio falls below 75th percentile relative to 10-year
Slope inverts or flattens below +5 bps per duration unit
Flight-to-quality event detected (money market spreads spike)

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. (unspecified), p. 64, Box 2: Front-End Treasury Richness
raw_chunk_text:
Box 2. Front-End Treasury Richness
Average Sharpe ratios are especially high at short maturities, and the reward–risk
curve is very steep at the front end (with longer maturities providing higher retu
rule_text: Extend duration from short-maturity Treasury bills into longer maturities when the reward-risk curve is steep and natural holders are under-optimizing for return, creating a persistent richness at the front end.
entry_conditions: 1-month Treasury Sharpe ratio > 75th percentile vs. 10-year Treasury Sharpe ratio
Reward-risk curve slope > +10 bps per duration unit
Relative value: (10Y yield - 1M yield) > 150 bps AND slope steepening YoY
exit_conditions: 1-month Sharpe ratio falls below 50th percentile relative to 10-year
Slope compresses below +8 bps per duration unit
Yield spread compresses below 100 bps

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. (unspecified), p. 64, Box 2: Front-End Treasury Richness
raw_chunk_text:
Box 2. Front-End Treasury Richness
Average Sharpe ratios are especially high at short maturities, and the reward–risk
curve is very steep at the front end (with longer maturities providing higher retu
rule_text: Extend duration from short-maturity Treasury bills into longer maturities when the reward-risk curve is steep and natural holders are under-optimizing for return, creating a persistent richness at the front end.

Reward-risk curve slope > +5 bps per duration unit

Crisis regime detected (VIX > 30 OR TED spread > 100 bps)

raw_chunk_text:
premium had become more or less the standard model.1 Typically, in the modern
period, the expected return on equities is obtained through a DDM. This
practice represents a counterrevolution or return
rule_text: Investors should only act on time-varying expected returns if they possess reliable predictive tools; without them, a buy-and-hold strategy at high risk premiums is preferable to active timing.
entry_conditions: Out-of-sample prediction accuracy > 60% on holdout test set
Information coefficient > 0.15 on lagged expected return forecasts
Sharpe ratio of timing strategy > 1.0 over 3+ year backtest
exit_conditions: Prediction accuracy falls below 55% on rolling 1-year window
Information coefficient degrades below 0.12
Hold-and-buy-dips Sharpe ratio exceeds active timing Sharpe ratio for 2 consecutive periods

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. ?, pp. 6-156

premium had become more or less the standard model.1 Typically, in the modern
period, the expected return on equities is obtained through a DDM. This
practice represents a counterrevolution or return
rule_text: Investors should only act on time-varying expected returns if they possess reliable predictive tools; without them, a buy-and-hold strategy at high risk premiums is preferable to active timing.

rule_text: Investors should only act on time-varying expected returns if they possess reliable predictive tools; without them, a buy-and-hold strategy at high risk premiums is preferable to active timing.

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.0, p.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
and risk factors.
• Strategy styles. The strategy style perspective is important for understanding
the profit potential of popular active trading approaches. The book drills
into value, carry, momentu
rule_text: Allocate capital to strategy styles (value, carry, momentum, asymmetric payoff selling) that have demonstrated consistent long-run outperformance comparable to the equity premium across diverse investment contexts.
entry_conditions: Strategy style has outperformed buy-and-hold in all 4+ tested market regimes
Outperformance magnitude ≥ 1.5x equity premium threshold
Minimum 20-year historical performance track record
Valuation/yield/momentum metric in top quintile of historical distribution
exit_conditions: Strategy metric falls below 40th percentile
Outperformance disappears in any major market regime for 3+ years

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Pages 6-156
raw_chunk_text:
forward-looking analysis
Perhaps the most common investor mistake is chasing returns by over-
weighting stocks, sectors, asset classes, or strategies that have been successful
in recent years. In fact
rule_text: Avoid extrapolating recent multi-year performance trends into expected returns; instead, weight forward-looking indicators (yields, valuations) more heavily than recent historical returns when forming expected return judgments.
entry_conditions: Asset/sector has outperformed for 3+ years
Forward-looking yield or valuation metric is below historical median
Reversion probability indicator (mean-reversion score) > 0.65
exit_conditions: Forward-looking metric reverts to historical mean
Position reaches +10% profit target

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Pages 6-156

Reversion probability indicator > 0.55
exit_conditions: Forward-looking metric approaches historical mean
Position reaches +15% profit target

such as extrapolation and overconfidence). The expected return on any invest-

A central insight from finance theories that
rule_text: Require higher risk premia for assets with losses correlated to bad times; require lower premia for safe-haven assets uncorrelated to systemic stress.

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.0 (Introduction), pp.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
negative risk premia. Strategies that resemble selling financial catastrophe insur-
ance (such as index volatility selling or carry-seeking strategies characterized by
steady small gains punctuated by
rule_text: Avoid or heavily discount strategies that generate steady small gains punctuated by infrequent but large losses (negative skew, tail risk), as they require exceptionally high risk premia compensation and concentrate losses in worst-case market regimes.
entry_conditions: Identify if strategy exhibits carry/vol-selling characteristics
Calculate Value-at-Risk (VaR) at 95th percentile loss
Calculate expected gain per period
Compare: (expected_annual_premium / var_95_loss) >= 3.0
exit_conditions: Exit immediately if ratio falls below 3.0
Exit if realized losses exceed 2x historical VaR
Exit on first month of negative returns

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.0 (Introduction), pp.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
negative risk premia. Strategies that resemble selling financial catastrophe insur-
ance (such as index volatility selling or carry-seeking strategies characterized by
steady small gains punctuated by
rule_text: Avoid or heavily discount strategies that generate steady small gains punctuated by infrequent but large losses (negative skew, tail risk), as they require exceptionally high risk premia compensation and concentrate losses in worst-case market regimes.
entry_conditions: Identify strategy skew profile using rolling returns (120-day window)
Calculate conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) at 90th percentile
Measure realized skewness; enter only if skewness < -0.5
Compare: (expected_annual_premium / cvar_90_loss) >= 2.0
exit_conditions: Exit if ratio falls below 2.0 for 5 consecutive periods
Exit if realized losses exceed CVaR by more than 50%
Exit if skewness deteriorates (becomes more negative)

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.0 (Introduction), pp.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
negative risk premia. Strategies that resemble selling financial catastrophe insur-
ance (such as index volatility selling or carry-seeking strategies characterized by
steady small gains punctuated by
rule_text: Avoid or heavily discount strategies that generate steady small gains punctuated by infrequent but large losses (negative skew, tail risk), as they require exceptionally high risk premia compensation and concentrate losses in worst-case market regimes.
entry_conditions: Measure strategy expected return vs. realized tail loss volatility
Calculate max drawdown from rolling 252-day windows
Compute Sortino ratio (only penalizes downside)
Compare: (expected_return / mean_tail_loss) >= 1.5
Apply leverage factor: 1 + (excess_premium / cvar)
exit_conditions: Exit if ratio falls below 1.5
Exit if drawdown exceeds 20% from peak
Reduce leverage if volatility of losses increases >20% YoY

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.0, p.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
of constant expected returns.5 The boom–bust cycles of the 2000s have helped
to cause both academic and practitioner views to shift toward accepting the idea
of time-varying expected returns. As a res
rule_text: A trader should employ market timing with humility and restraint, sizing tactical positions modestly due to the inherent difficulty of forecasting and the noise in expected return estimates, especially given that market-timing trades lack breadth.
entry_conditions: Expected return forecast exists with statistical confidence
Forecast confidence interval width < 2% annualized return
Time horizon at least 6 months (to avoid 'right but early' problem)
exit_conditions: Original forecast thesis invalidated
Time horizon elapsed with position unproven
Position drawdown exceeds 1.5% of portfolio

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.1, p.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
Moreover, market-timing trades lack breadth,6 and being “right but early”
involves substantial career risk. Finally, systematic contrarian trading signals
often disagree starkly with discretionary tim
rule_text: Allocate only a minority portion of risk budget to tactical market-timing trades, while maintaining non-zero exposure to exploit time-varying expected returns, to balance career risk against opportunity capture.
entry_conditions: Valuation signal indicates clear mispricing (>2 standard deviations from mean)
Systematic contrarian signal aligns with discretionary structural view
Risk budget availability confirmed
exit_conditions: Position drawdown exceeds 1% of portfolio
Holding period expires at 12 weeks
Conflicting signals emerge between valuation and structural views

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. 0, p. 6-156
raw_chunk_text:
time blurs the boundary between static holdings and dynamic strategies. Buy-and-hold strategies
are static (passive) in terms of assets, but they allow changing characteristics over time.
Conversely,
rule_text: To maintain stable portfolio characteristics and factor exposures over time, a trader must periodically rebalance holdings because market movements cause characteristics to drift, and ex ante rewards for those characteristics are time-varying.
entry_conditions: Factor exposure has drifted ±15% or more from target weight
Ex ante reward indicator (e.g., factor premium signal) has shifted regime over 60-day lookback period
exit_conditions: Rebalance completed; position held until next drift threshold breach

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets by Antti Ilmanen, Chapter on Expected Returns, pp. 6-156
raw_chunk_text:
6Breadth is the ability to diversify the impact of a given beat-the-market insight across multiple
bets (say, by buying a wide variety of stocks believed to be cheap). See Grinold and Kahn (2000).
5
E
rule_text: Enhance long-run returns by collecting risk premia from diverse sources rather than concentrating on single premium sources; breadth of diversification amplifies the impact of investment insights.
entry_conditions: Identify risk premium source with strong historical support
Allocate capital only when premium signal strength > 60th percentile
exit_conditions: Exit when premium signal strength falls below 40th percentile
Rebalance when any single source exceeds 45% of allocated capital

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. 0, p.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
Equity and illiquidity premia are good return sources but should not
dominate the portfolio, especially at times when their ex ante rewards are
slim. Entry and exit valuations matter.
• Investors can
rule_text: A trader should moderate exposure to high-volatility return sources and apply leverage judiciously to diversify across multiple return sources (value, carry, momentum) rather than concentrating on a single strategy, while adjusting position sizing based on ex ante reward levels.
entry_conditions: Identify 3+ return sources with measurable ex ante rewards
Ex ante Sharpe ratio for each source > 0.4
Sum of portfolio weights across all sources ≤ 100% (no leverage)
High-volatility assets comprise ≤ 20% of portfolio
exit_conditions: Ex ante reward drops below 0.4 Sharpe for any active source
Portfolio concentration in single source exceeds 35%
Realized volatility of portfolio exceeds 8% annualized

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. 0, p.6-156
raw_chunk_text:
Equity and illiquidity premia are good return sources but should not
dominate the portfolio, especially at times when their ex ante rewards are
slim. Entry and exit valuations matter.
• Investors can
rule_text: A trader should moderate exposure to high-volatility return sources and apply leverage judiciously to diversify across multiple return sources (value, carry, momentum) rather than concentrating on a single strategy, while adjusting position sizing based on ex ante reward levels.
entry_conditions: Identify 2+ return sources with measurable ex ante rewards
Ex ante Sharpe ratio for each source > 0.25
Apply 1.2x-1.5x leverage to achieve diversification benefits
High-volatility assets comprise ≤ 35% of portfolio
exit_conditions: Ex ante reward drops below 0.25 Sharpe for any active source
Portfolio concentration in single source exceeds 45%
Realized volatility of portfolio exceeds 12% annualized

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. 0, p.6-156

entry_conditions: Identify 2+ return sources with positive ex ante expected returns

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.6-156, p.6
raw_chunk_text:
models, investors do not fully know the structure of the economic system,
so they must gradually learn about the unknown structural parameters. The
premium for such uncertainty can be an order of magn
rule_text: Demand a higher risk premium for assets exposed to structural uncertainty (political risk, regulatory change, unknown economic parameters) because the uncertainty premium can be an order of magnitude larger than premiums in well-understood models.
entry_conditions: Asset is NOT exposed to active political/regulatory regime uncertainty
Structural parameters of economic environment are well-documented and stable
Historical volatility of policy regime is low (1-year rolling std < 15%)
exit_conditions: Structural uncertainty premium declines below 300 bps
Regulatory environment materially changes in any direction
Political risk indicators rise

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch.6-156, p.6
raw_chunk_text:
models, investors do not fully know the structure of the economic system,
so they must gradually learn about the unknown structural parameters. The
premium for such uncertainty can be an order of magn
rule_text: Demand a higher risk premium for assets exposed to structural uncertainty (political risk, regulatory change, unknown economic parameters) because the uncertainty premium can be an order of magnitude larger than premiums in well-understood models.
entry_conditions: Asset is exposed to some political or regulatory regime uncertainty
Structural uncertainty premium is between 150-200 bps above baseline
Policy volatility is measurable but not extreme (15-30% rolling std)
exit_conditions: Structural uncertainty premium falls below 150 bps
Policy volatility regime shifts materially (>5% change in rolling std)
Realized policy changes exceed 2 standard deviations of historical norm

so they must gradually learn about the unknown structural parameters. The
premium for such uncertainty can be an order of magn
rule_text: Demand a higher risk premium for assets exposed to structural uncertainty (political risk, regulatory change, unknown economic parameters) because the uncertainty premium can be an order of magnitude larger than premiums in well-understood models.
entry_conditions: Asset faces identifiable but evolving structural uncertainty (political/regulatory regime shift)
Structural uncertainty premium exceeds 75 bps but market recognition is still incomplete
Policy volatility is elevated relative to historical 2-year average (30%+ rolling std)
exit_conditions: Structural uncertainty premium compresses to 50 bps or below
Policy regime stabilizes (rolling volatility drops below 20%)
Single adverse event triggers regime shift beyond 3 std deviations

rule_text: Align portfolio evaluation frequency and risk exposure to investment time horizon; longer horizons justify higher risky asset allocation because the probability of outperformance improves and loss aversion effects diminish over extended periods.
entry_conditions: Investment horizon >= 12 months
Portfolio evaluation frequency <= annual (252+ trading days between rebalance)
Risky asset allocation target set at baseline level
exit_conditions: Rebalance on calendar schedule (annual)
Deviation from target allocation > 10%

rule_text: Adjust position sizing and risk appetite dynamically based on recent portfolio performance, being more aggressive after gains (perceiving them as 'house money') and more conservative after losses.
entry_conditions: Calculate 20-day rolling portfolio return
If rolling return > +5%, classify as 'house money' state
If rolling return < -5%, classify as 'loss aversion' state
exit_conditions: Exit when rolling return crosses threshold back to neutral zone (-5% to +5%)
Or use standard profit target/stop loss independent of regime

rule_text: Rebalance allocations between correlated asset classes when relative weights deviate from target, because extreme weight concentrations have negative correlation with subsequent relative performance.

12 months have elapsed since last rebalance
exit_conditions: Rebalance executed; weights returned to 50/50

source_citation: Expected Returns on Major Assets, Antti Ilmanen, Ch. 2, pp. 6-156
raw_chunk_text:
and French (2002), and Ibbotson and Chen (2003). Each study uses a slightly
different method of removing the impact of unexpected windfall gains to
recover the equity risk premium that was expected ov
rule_text: Reduce expected equity risk premium forecasts when current market valuations are materially higher than historical averages, as forward-looking returns should adjust downward from the long-term historical equity-bond risk premium of ~4%.
entry_conditions: Current PE ratio or valuation metric exceeds historical median by 10%
Forward-looking expected equity risk premium falls below 3.5%
exit_conditions: Valuation returns to within 10% of historical mean
Expected risk premium recovers above 4%
